step.title: Step 23
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN moves player down toward target A')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: UP works when player is outside chamber column! Player moved from rows 32-35 to rows 29-31. Player now near target A but can't move UP further. Player at rows 29-31, cols 48-51; target A at rows 24-27, cols 44-47.
- Plan: Move LEFT to align with target A, then try UP to reach target A's row level.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After moving LEFT, the player jumped from rows 29-31 to rows 20-23, and from cols 48-51 to cols 45-47. That's a big jump!
The player is now at rows 20-23, cols 45-47. Target A is at rows 24-27, cols 44-47. So the player is above target A!
The player's cols 45-47 overlap with target A's cols 44-47. So the player is aligned with target A horizontally.
But the player is at rows 20-23, which is above target A's rows 24-27. So the player is 3-4 rows above target A.
The UP action didn't move the player further. The player stayed at rows 20-23.
Now I need to get the player to the same row level as target A (rows 24-27). Since the player is above target A, I need to move DOWN.
Let me try DOWN to move the player down to target A's row level.
Actually, wait. Let me think about the movement pattern:
- RIGHT: moves player right and up (diagonal)
- LEFT: moves player left and... up? The player went from rows 29-31 to rows 20-23, which is UP by 9 rows. And from cols 48-51 to cols 45-47, which is LEFT by 3-4 cols.
- UP: moves player up (when not blocked)
- DOWN: should move player down?
So LEFT also moves the player up! This is confusing. Let me try DOWN to move the player down to target A's row level.
Actually, looking at the image, the player (green) is at the top of the board, above target A. The player needs to move DOWN to reach target A.
Let me try DOWN.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: LEFT also moves player diagonally up-left! Player jumped from rows 29-31 to rows 20-23. Player now at rows 20-23, cols 45-47, which is above target A (rows 24-27, cols 44-47). Player aligned with target A horizontally.
Plan: Move DOWN to reach target A's row level, then press SPACE to destroy target A.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try DOWN to move toward target A
predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN moves player down toward target A')
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player after DOWN: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Check target A
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Target A: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}]")

# Try DOWN again if needed
if seg['nodes'][0]['color'] != 'N':
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'N':
            b = n['boundary']
            rows = [p[0] for p in b]
            if min(rows) < 24:
                predict(expect_change=True, note='DOWN again')
                action(['DOWN'])
                for n2 in seg['nodes']:
                    if n2['color'] == 'N':
                        b2 = n2['boundary']
                        rows2 = [p[0] for p in b2]
                        cols2 = [p[1] for p in b2]
                        print(f"Player after 2nd DOWN: rows [{min(rows2)}-{max(rows2)}], cols [{min(cols2)}-{max(cols2)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Player after DOWN: rows [16-18], cols [44-47]
Target A: rows [24-27], cols [44-47]
Target A: rows [63-63], cols [52-63]
Player after 2nd DOWN: rows [16-18], cols [44-47]
